hard.next_step: 20th June - Year 1 licences invoice issued. 18th June - Sales order completed, PO shared with finance. 17th June - Full signed copy received. 12th June - signed signature page received; CPO confirmed signing. SOW workshop held 29 Jun; invoice-timing dispute opened 23-26 Jun (Mair wants billing at go-live, Carl offered 60-day terms).

north_star_verdict.headline: The sale is essentially won: MSA and Order Form 1 fully signed (signature page 12 Jun, full copy 17 Jun), PO 4800686635 received 18 Jun, Year-1 licence invoice issued 20 Jun and the SOW workshop held 29 Jun. It stays Close Date Risk rather than clean On Track for two live post-signature frictions: Mair is disputing the upfront licence invoice (Mazin and Ranjit want billing to start at UAT/go-live, roughly four months out, on the 3 Jul call; Carl is taking it internally and offered 60-day terms), and account ownership moved from Dan Quinn to Mohamad Alhakim on 1 Jul mid-transaction. The 28 May close date is long past; the real milestone (signature) landed mid-June.
north_star_verdict.math: Today 6 Jul 2026. Close date 28 May 2026 passed 39 days ago; the real milestone (full signature) landed 12-17 Jun, so the placeholder close slipped ~20 days against signature, ~39 days against the field. Deal age 231 days (created 17 Nov 2025). ~24 days in Contract In Progress since the 12 Jun signature. End-to-end 12 Nov 2025 discovery to 17 Jun 2026 execution (~7 months) is reasonable for a $265k first-sale enterprise deal with an RFP, three-ERP scoping and legal redlines. Remaining path: settle the invoice-timing terms and run Phase 1 kickoff after SOW signature.
evidence: Contract fully executed: signed signature page received 12 Jun 2026, full initialled/stamped/signed copy received 17 Jun 2026 (source: Next Step; email 'Signed Agreement - PO4800686635' from glenice.keen@mairgroup.com, 17 Jun).
PO 4800686635 received and Year-1 licence invoice No. 141/DXB-2026-27 issued 18-20 Jun 2026 (source: Next Step; Clari task emails 18-23 Jun).
SOW workshop held 29 Jun 2026 with full Mair and Zycus teams (source: event 'SOW Workshop - See Agenda & Required Attendees Below', 29 Jun; email thread 18-25 Jun).

Invoice-timing friction live on the 3 Jul 2026 call: Mazin says 'we don't get invoiced ... until we, as the company, are starting to actually play with the environment ... typically in the UAT stage' (~4 months in); Ranjit adds 'we are ADX compliant ... we need to confirm receipt and acceptance of the services, then only finance can pay'; Carl to revert 'by Monday or Tuesday next week' (source: Avoma 3 Jul 2026).

Legal jurisdiction resolved 10 Jun 2026: Mair board resolution mandated exclusive Abu Dhabi courts (Omar: 'it is a board resolution ... we cannot accept any arbitration'); Zycus VP Legal Zoheb conceded and deleted the arbitration clause to finalize (source: Avoma 10 Jun 2026).
Account ownership changed Dan Quinn to Mohamad Alhakim (Regional VP) on 1 Jul 2026; Carl introduced Mohamad to the Mair team on 26 Jun (source: field history 2026-07-01; Clari email 26 Jun).
Buyer engagement is live: incoming emails 29 Jun and a 'Mair Group/ Zycus project' call on 3 Jul 2026, Last Activity Date 3 Jul (source: activities).

metrics.narrative: The business problem is well established and buyer-articulated: Mair Group is a five-company merger trying to harmonize fragmented procurement across three ERPs (SAP S/4HANA, SAP B1 and Yardi), and Mazin Abdulhadi Hawasli flagged on the 21 Apr 2026 call that his team was still 'submitting hard copies, which I said I have never done in over a decade and a half', framing the transformation mandate. The evaluation ran on price, implementation phasing and SaaS-vs-on-premise considerations, not on quantified savings: across six months of negotiation the buyer never put a dollar ROI or savings target on record (source: AIS_Why__c). The value is therefore qualitative (cycle-time and standardization), so quantifying the baseline (procurement cycle time, supplier-onboarding days, spend under management) with Ranjit and Mazin during Phase 1 scoping is the move to firm the business case for the reference story.

economic_buyer.narrative: Mazin Abdulhadi Hawasli, Director of Procurement, is the economic buyer: he ran the commercial demand ('at the end, Dan, I will be pulling out a big baseball bat and asking you to give me a nice little reduction in price', 21 Apr call), countersigned the agreement on behalf of Mair Group ('on behalf of Mair Group will be myself', 10 Jun legal call), and personally re-opened the invoice-timing terms on 3 Jul. Budget authority sits above him with a public-listed board (the arbitration clause was a board resolution Omar and Mazin could not override), and Vishal Kapil (Group CIO & CTO, Decision Maker) co-owns the technical sign-off. Mazin is bought-in and reachable (email 29 Jun, call 3 Jul); the forward action is keeping him aligned on the billing compromise so Finance releases the licence payment.

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are clear from the call sequence: pre-built integration to their three ERPs (SAP S/4HANA, SAP B1 and Yardi) was the make-or-break, with Vishal warning on 21 Apr that 'integration is one aspect which either makes or breaks a project' and the inflexible Yardi connection being the top delivery risk. Phased implementation (Sourcing/PR-to-PO first, then supplier management, then contract and invoice) over big-bang was a hard buyer requirement (3 Apr and 6 May calls), as was commercial competitiveness against GEP (priced ~50% below Zycus). SaaS fit-for-purpose, regional Middle East presence and reference-ability in GCC retail rounded out the criteria. Zycus met all of them on paper terms, phasing and connectors; the only criterion it could not fully match was RTO/RPO/uptime versus the competition (9 Apr note), which the champion judged 'won't be a showstopper'.

decision_process.narrative: The process the buyer actually ran is fully visible in the call and email sequence: RFP hand-delivered to Abu Dhabi HQ 6 Mar, clarifications through Mar, preferred-vendor confirmed 3 Apr, F2F commercial negotiation 21 Apr, BAFO and 5-year term agreed, LOI 8 May, MSA/Order Form redlines through May, a legal-to-legal jurisdiction call 10 Jun, signature 12-17 Jun, PO and licence invoice 18-20 Jun, and the SOW workshop 29 Jun. The approval chain is heavily governed: procurement (Mazin/Lovemer) plus IT (Vishal/Ranjit) plus a Head of Legal (Omar) plus a board resolution on jurisdiction, and a three-way-match finance control ('confirm receipt and acceptance of the services, then only finance can pay', Ranjit, 3 Jul). Signatories: Carl Kimball signed for Zycus; Mazin countersigned for Mair; the SOW is signed separately to trigger delivery kickoff.

paper_process.narrative: Paper mechanics are essentially done and are the strongest part of the deal. Whose-paper was contested (Mair's default is their own board-approved template; Zycus argued its SaaS-fit paper was faster) and resolved in Zycus's favour after Head of Legal Omar agreed to review Zycus paper. The single blocking redline was dispute resolution: Mair's board resolution mandated exclusive Abu Dhabi courts with no arbitration, and Zycus VP Legal Zoheb conceded the arbitration clause on the 10 Jun call to close it. MSA plus Order Form 1 were then signed via DocuSign (Carl for Zycus, Mazin for Mair), PO 4800686635 issued, and vendor registration (Trade Licence, TRN, bank details) completed 27 Apr. The remaining paper is the SOW (workshop held 29 Jun, signed separately by the AVP Global Delivery to start kickoff) plus Order Form 2 for advanced support, and the open invoice-timing terms.
sources: Avoma legal call 10 Jun 2026 (Omar, Zoheb, Mazin)

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is concrete and buyer-owned: Mair is 'born out of a 5 different company merger' with fragmented, manual procurement, and Mazin is trying to 'harmonize into one way of doing things' while restructuring the department (3 Jul: 'this solution ... is due to us restructuring the department'). Procurement was still submitting hard copies to vendors, which Mazin called embarrassingly outdated on 21 Apr. The transformation/process pain owner is Mazin (Director of Procurement); the technical pain owner is Vishal Kapil (Group CIO & CTO), who owns the multi-ERP integration risk and singled out the inflexible Yardi/American real-estate system as the likeliest source of delay ('they are very, very inflexible ... I see the risk right away in the plan', 21 Apr).

champion.narrative: The deal closed on strong economic sponsorship rather than a classic coaching champion. Vishal Kapil (Group CIO & CTO, Decision Maker) is a credible technical validator with prior Zycus exposure who joined every major session, but he is a validator, not an internal seller. Mazin (Director of Procurement, economic buyer) drove the deal to signature, yet he was making the decision, not coaching Zycus. Lovemer Raymundo is the most frequent Mair-side contact and the operational lead, and Ranjit Warang (Senior Manager - SAP) is driving the SOW/kickoff scheduling and even flagged RFP integration nuances helpfully. What is developed: clear sponsorship and a signed contract; what is still missing: an internal advocate who defends Zycus and navigates escalation if Phase 1 delivery stumbles. Building Lovemer and Ranjit into that role with early Phase 1 wins is the forward action, but on a signed, executing deal this is a Phase-1 watch-item, not a deal risk.
sources: OpportunityContactRole (Vishal Kapil, Ranjit Warang, Lovemer Raymundo)

competition.narrative: Competition is effectively over: the contract is signed and executing. GEP Worldwide was the single live peer threat (priced ~50% below Zycus per the 3 Apr Next Step note), but Mair informally confirmed Zycus as preferred vendor on 3 Apr, issued the LOI 8 May and signed 12-17 Jun, so GEP is out. Coupa (Mazin's prior 16-country rollout experience, kept out here) and SAP Ariba (licences written off) are historical context; Ivalua, JAGGAER and Oracle are Competitors__c awareness-set names never raised on a call; Zip was name-dropped by Mazin as a market alternative, not a live evaluation. Zycus won on speed-to-value via pre-built SAP/Yardi connectors, unified three-ERP experience, regional Middle East presence and SaaS-fit paper. The only residual competitive risk is a GEP re-approach if Phase 1 delivery falters, which is why protecting the go-live matters.

items.text: On the 3 Jul call Mazin and Ranjit pushed to defer the licence invoice to the UAT stage (~4 months out) citing a three-way-match finance control, and Carl committed to come back with a compromise by early next week.

The deal is signed at $265,570 on a 5-year term after Zycus discounted implementation by over 25 percent, but the upfront Year-1 licence invoice is now contested and internal delivery flagged an unfunded $60k advanced-support carve-out that still needs an Order Form 2 fix.

Mohamad Alhakim (Regional VP) took ownership on 1 Jul mid-transaction and is now the Zycus lead into Mazin; Carl introduced him on 26 Jun and he chaired the 3 Jul call, so the relationship is fresh but engaged.
No active competitor: GEP was the only live peer at ~50 percent of Zycus pricing and is out since the 17 Jun signature, leaving only a GEP re-approach risk if Phase 1 delivery stumbles.

deal_movement.summary: The deal moved from redlines to fully executed and into delivery over 10 Jun to 3 Jul: jurisdiction resolved, MSA/OF1 signed, PO and licence invoice issued, SOW workshop held. Two fresh frictions are live (invoice-timing dispute, mid-deal owner change). Buyer engagement is current (call 3 Jul).

items.change: Legal jurisdiction resolved: Zycus conceded arbitration clause, exclusive Abu Dhabi courts accepted per Mair board resolution
MSA and Order Form 1 fully signed (signature page 12 Jun, full initialled/stamped copy 17 Jun)
PO 4800686635 received and Year-1 licence invoice No. 141/DXB-2026-27 issued
SOW workshop held with full Mair and Zycus teams
Invoice-timing dispute opened: Mair wants billing to start at UAT/go-live; Carl offered 60-day payment terms as compromise
Account ownership transferred Dan Quinn to Mohamad Alhakim (Regional VP)
Invoice-timing call held: Carl to revert with a compromise by early next week

competitive_position.summary: No active competitor as of 6 Jul 2026: the deal is signed and executing. GEP Worldwide was the only live peer (priced ~50% below Zycus per the 3 Apr 2026 note) and is OUT since Zycus was selected 3 Apr and signed 17 Jun. Coupa (kept out; Mazin's prior 16-country experience) and SAP Ariba (licences written off) are historical; Ivalua, JAGGAER and Oracle are awareness-set names never raised on calls; Zip was a market name-drop, not an evaluation. The only residual threat is a GEP re-approach if Phase 1 delivery stumbles.

competitors.name: GEP Worldwide
competitors.quote: Confirmed informally we are the preferred vendor and are in competition with GEP. Note GEP are 50% of our pricing in the Al Tasnim opp.
competitors.how_we_win: Speed-to-value via pre-built SAP B1/S4HANA/Yardi connectors vs GEP's custom build; unified three-ERP experience; regional Middle East team; SaaS-fit contracting. Protect the win by keeping Phase 1 green so GEP has no re-entry.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: I have not let Coupa walk in through the door. Because in my previous work, I had 16 country rollout for Coupa, and the pricing I had from Coupa is similar to what you are offering for one single country.
competitors.how_we_win: Mazin deliberately kept Coupa out; Zycus positioned as simpler and fit for the three-ERP environment at competitive pricing.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: Ariba licences acquired but written off.
competitors.how_we_win: Ariba is unused inventory; Mair moved on before Zycus engaged.

competitors.name: Ivalua
competitors.source: SF Competitors__c field only; never raised on calls or Next Step
competitors.how_we_win: Awareness-set name only; no active evaluation.

competitors.name: JAGGAER
competitors.source: SF Competitors__c field only; never raised on calls or Next Step
competitors.how_we_win: Awareness-set name only; no active evaluation.

competitors.name: Oracle
competitors.source: SF Competitors__c field only; never raised on calls or Next Step
competitors.how_we_win: Awareness-set name only; no active evaluation.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Mair's stated expectations (pre-built three-ERP integration, phased single-contract delivery, SaaS-fit paper, competitive commercials, GCC retail reference-ability) are all met, with two soft exposures: RTO/RPO/uptime could not fully match the competition, and billing timing is contested against their three-way-match finance policy.

items.criterion: Pre-built integration to SAP S/4HANA, SAP B1 and Yardi
items.quote: Integration is one aspect which either makes or breaks a project ... the inflexible Yardi/American real-estate system is the risk I see right away in the plan.

items.criterion: Phased implementation over big-bang, single contract
items.quote: Looking to move away from big bang to a phased approach focussing on priority modules e.g. Sourcing first ... have indicated it will still be a single contract.

items.criterion: Use of Zycus SaaS-fit paper vs Mair's standard template
items.quote: Mair head of legal has agreed to continue reviewing our paper; jurisdiction resolved to Abu Dhabi courts and arbitration clause deleted.

items.criterion: RTO/RPO and uptime matching the competition
items.quote: Some issues where we were not able to match GEP i.e. RTO/RPO (concerns due to current war situation and AWS taken out in Dubai & Bahrain) - champion thinks it won't be a showstopper.

items.criterion: Billing timed to value realization (UAT/go-live) per three-way-match finance policy
items.quote: We need to confirm receipt and acceptance of the services, then only finance can pay ... value realization will start at least initial go live.

items.requirement: Align the licence invoice to value realization at UAT/go-live, not upfront
items.quote: Typically ... we arrange with our service providers that the invoicing will kick off when we, as the company, are starting to actually play with the environment ... which is typically in the UAT stage.

items.requirement: Schedule the SOW workshop and formal Phase 1 project kickoff with the sponsor
items.quote: Let's initiate a formal project kick-off session with the project sponsor and key stakeholders next week, followed by sessions on the topics mentioned below.

items.deliverable: Revert to Mazin with an invoice-timing compromise aligned to UAT/payment terms
items.grounding_quote: Give me a couple of days, and I'll figure it out ... maybe by Monday or Tuesday next week, and I'll get.

items.deliverable: Issue the phase-split payment-milestone schedule with cost broken down by phase
items.grounding_quote: Hrishi is going to rewrite the payment schedule ... splitting out the design sign off into two different phases ... also the commercials, a breakdown of the cost by phases.

items.deliverable: Confirm SOW-workshop follow-up dates and sign the SOW to trigger delivery kickoff
items.grounding_quote: As per Zycus internal processes, Delivery team engagement begins once SOW is signed, this signature will start the kick off meeting.

items.deliverable: Confirm acceptance of the 60-day payment-terms compromise so Finance releases the licence payment
items.grounding_quote: I have been able to secure 1st payment terms for 60 days, if we can raise invoice now with the payment due in 60 days - I think is best compromise.

items.area: Advanced-support revenue absorbed into SaaS with no delivery approval
items.quote: 60k USD was provided as part of the advanced support ... but that's not possible because I have to add one person for this ... it should have come to approval to either me, Daniel or Chiru.

items.area: Quantified ROI/savings baseline for the business case
items.quote: Zero buyer-side or rep-side value quantification; evaluated on price and implementation phasing.

best_practice_check.summary: A first-sale, signed deal now in delivery: the win-protection levers are settling the invoice-timing terms cleanly, closing the internal advanced-support pricing gap, and building an operational champion to keep Phase 1 green so GEP has no re-entry.
flags: Close the invoice-timing dispute fast and in writing: Mair's three-way-match policy and the mid-deal owner change make a clean billing compromise the single highest-impact lever to protect cash and goodwill.
Fix the unfunded $60k advanced-support carve-out via Order Form 2 before SOW sign-off, so Delivery has funded headcount and the SOW is not held up internally.

Convert Lovemer Raymundo and Ranjit Warang into active Phase 1 champions with early wins, since the deal closed on economic sponsorship without a coaching champion and Yardi integration is the top delivery risk.

items.name: Mazin Abdulhadi Hawasli
items.title: Director of Procurement
items.risk: Set the invoice-timing pushback and countersigned; alignment on billing is the live watch-item.

items.name: Vishal Kapil
items.title: Group CIO & CTO
items.risk: Owns multi-ERP integration risk (SAP + Yardi); keep close during Phase 1 scoping.

champion_strength.summary: The deal closed on strong economic sponsorship rather than a classic coaching champion. Vishal Kapil (Group CIO & CTO, Decision Maker) is a credible technical validator who joined every major session but is a validator, not an internal seller; Mazin (Director of Procurement) drove the deal to signature as the economic buyer, not as a coach. Lovemer Raymundo (operational lead) and Ranjit Warang (SAP owner, actively helpful on integration) are the best candidates to build into active champions with early Phase 1 wins. At this signed, executing stage the absence of a coaching champion is a Phase-1 execution watch-item, not a deal risk.
champion_strength.champion: Vishal Kapil (Group CIO & CTO, Decision Maker) is the closest technical champion; Lovemer Raymundo and Ranjit Warang are the operational leads to develop into advocates.
champion_strength.source: OpportunityContactRole (Vishal Kapil, Ranjit Warang, Lovemer Raymundo) + Avoma calls Apr-Jun 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: No AI/Merlin content in scope or in any call across Nov 2025 to Jul 2026; product scope is iContract, iSource and eProcurement with no agentic or Merlin SKU. This is a traditional procurement-automation deal, correctly positioned; there is no AI story to under-position.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 2.0 / Negative is accurate: six months of late-stage negotiation detail carry zero buyer or rep AI mention, and no Merlin/agentic SKU is in scope. Evaluated purely on price, phasing and SaaS-vs-on-premise. Phase 2/3 (contract and invoice modules) could open a future Merlin intake/analytics conversation, but there is no current appetite.

items.detail: Mair's three-way-match finance policy resists the upfront Year-1 licence invoice; they want billing to start at UAT/go-live (~4 months out), risking a cash-timing standoff if no compromise lands.
items.status: open, Carl to revert early next week

items.detail: The 28 May close date is 39 days past; signature landed mid-June and the SOW sign-off plus kickoff are the remaining forcing steps, so the forecast date needs correcting to the delivery timeline.
Internal delivery flagged an unfunded $60k advanced-support scope absorbed into SaaS with no headcount approval; needs an Order Form 2 fix before SOW sign-off.
Yardi (inflexible third-party US real-estate ERP) is the top Phase 1 delivery risk flagged by Vishal; SIT and people-availability over the summer could put the plan in red.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is live and warm: signed contract, PO and invoice issued, SOW workshop held, incoming buyer emails 29 Jun and a call 3 Jul. The only cooling risk would be an unresolved billing standoff souring the new-customer relationship.
items: Buyer-initiated meeting invitations and a live 3 Jul call show a pull, not a push.
New Zycus owner Mohamad Alhakim engaged directly with Mazin on 3 Jul within days of the 1 Jul handover.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: This first-sale $265k deal is signed and executing (MSA/OF1 signed 17 Jun, PO received, licence invoice issued, SOW workshop held 29 Jun); the only thing between us and a clean close is settling Mair's invoice-timing pushback and getting the SOW signed to trigger kickoff.

win_position.text: Win sits high because the contract is fully executed: Mazin countersigned for Mair and Carl for Zycus on 17 Jun, PO 4800686635 is in and the Year-1 licence invoice issued 20 Jun, so the award is won, not forecast.

Zycus beat the only live rival GEP (priced ~50% below us) on pre-built SAP/S4HANA/Yardi connectors, single-contract phasing and SaaS-fit paper, with Mazin having deliberately kept Coupa out of the evaluation.

The upfront licence invoice is contested: on 3 Jul Mazin and Ranjit invoked Mair's three-way-match finance policy to defer billing to UAT (~4 months out), so a mishandled compromise could sour cash timing and the new-customer relationship.
Account ownership changed from Dan Quinn to Mohamad Alhakim on 1 Jul mid-transaction, adding relationship-continuity risk right as the billing negotiation and Phase 1 kickoff need a steady hand.

deal_momentum.text: Momentum is strong and recent: jurisdiction resolved 10 Jun, signature 12-17 Jun, PO and invoice 18-20 Jun and the SOW workshop 29 Jun, with a live buyer call on 3 Jul.
Seniority and commercial topics are fully engaged, with a governed board-level legal process and a three-way-match finance control now driving the conversation.

customer_commitment.text: Mair committed to a 5-year term, signed the MSA/OF1, issued a PO and agreed to be a GCC retail reference customer ('having our reference in the retail space in the GCC will be some big leverage for you', Mazin, 3 Jul).
Commitment on payment timing is still soft: Finance will only release the licence payment on confirmed receipt/acceptance, so the invoice-terms compromise must be pinned in writing.

deal_risk.text: The invoice-timing standoff against Mair's finance policy is the live commercial risk; Carl owes a compromise by early next week.
Delivery risk concentrates on the inflexible Yardi integration and summer people-availability, plus an unfunded $60k advanced-support carve-out that needs an Order Form 2 fix before SOW sign-off.

close_plan_concretizing.evidence: Signed contract, PO, invoice and SOW workshop all landed within three weeks; only SOW sign-off and kickoff remain.

budget.evidence: Invoice-timing dispute against a three-way-match finance policy is unresolved as of 3 Jul.

customer_preference.evidence: Zycus selected as preferred vendor over GEP, signed a 5-year contract, and Mazin praised the partnership and committed to a GCC retail reference ('thank you for giving Zycus the trust ... our reference in retail in the GCC will be big leverage for you', 3 Jul).

business_case.evidence: Clear qualitative pain (five-company merger, manual hard-copy procurement, three-ERP harmonization) but no buyer-quantified ROI or savings; evaluated on price and phasing (AIS_Why__c).

scope_change.from: Source-to-Pay across sourcing, procurement and contract modules, big-bang
scope_change.to: Same S2P scope (iContract, iSource, eProcurement) delivered as a single contract in three phases, Sourcing/PR-to-PO first
scope_change.detail: Buyer shifted from big-bang to a phased 14-month rollout for delivery risk and bandwidth reasons, but kept a single contract at the full $265k scope, so this is a delivery-sequencing change, not a scope reduction.

items.action: Send Mazin a written invoice-timing compromise tying Year-1 payment to a fixed date near UAT.
items.trigger: Carl committed on the 3 Jul call to revert with a compromise by Monday/Tuesday; Mair's finance policy blocks upfront payment.
items.expected_effect: Unblocks the licence payment and protects the new-customer relationship without tying revenue to a variable go-live event.

items.action: Close the $60k advanced-support gap via an Order Form 2 line before SOW sign-off.
items.trigger: Delivery flagged the advanced-support scope was absorbed into SaaS with no funded headcount or approval.
items.expected_effect: Funds delivery headcount and removes an internal blocker to signing the SOW and starting Phase 1.

items.action: Lock SOW follow-up dates with Ranjit and get the SOW signed to trigger kickoff.
items.trigger: SOW workshop held 29 Jun; delivery engagement begins only once the SOW is signed.
items.expected_effect: Starts the Phase 1 clock and converts the signed contract into active delivery.

items.action: Run the Phase 1 kickoff with Mohamad, Mazin, Ranjit and Vishal, front-loading Yardi integration.
items.trigger: Vishal flagged the inflexible Yardi ERP and summer availability as the top delivery risks on 21 Apr.
items.expected_effect: De-risks the go-live, keeps GEP out, and builds Ranjit and Lovemer into active champions.

items.action: Capture Mazin's GCC retail reference commitment in writing after a clean Phase 1 milestone.
items.trigger: Mazin offered to be a retail reference in the GCC on the 3 Jul call.
items.expected_effect: Converts goodwill into a marquee reference and strengthens retention/expansion for the region.
